name: @moneyworks/cli
description: Command-line interface for testing and interacting with MoneyWorks Core
overview: This package provides a comprehensive CLI for developers working with MoneyWorks. It offers commands for data export/import, connection testing, and MWScript evaluation, with support for multiple output formats and flexible configuration options.

exports.name: Commands
exports.description: Available CLI commands for interacting with MoneyWorks

items.name: test-connection
items.description: Tests connection to MoneyWorks server and displays server information including version, document count, and sample data availability.
items.example:
mw test-connection
# Testing connection to localhost:6710...
# ✓ Connected to MoneyWorks 9.1.7
# ✓ Found 3 documents
# ✓ Found 5 tax rates in Acme Widgets

# With custom config
mw test-connection --config prod-config.json

# With debug output
mw test-connection --debug

items.name: export
items.description: Exports data from specified MoneyWorks table with support for filtering, pagination, and multiple output formats.
items.example:
# Basic export
mw export TaxRate

# Export with filter and save to file
mw export TaxRate --filter "Rate1 > 10" --output high-rates.json

# Export in compact format with limit
mw export Account --exportFormat compact --limit 100

# Export with ordering and pagination
mw export Transaction \
  --filter "TransDate >= '20250101'" \
  --orderBy "TransDate DESC" \
  --limit 50 \
  --offset 100

# Export with schema information
mw export Product --exportFormat schema --output product-schema.json

items.name: import
items.description: Imports data into MoneyWorks table from JSON file with support for different import modes and field calculation.
items.example:
# Basic import
mw import TaxRate --file new-rates.json

# Update existing records
mw import TaxRate --file updates.json --mode update

# Create or update based on key
mw import Account --file accounts.json --mode createOrUpdate

# Import with calculated fields
mw import TaxRate --file rates.json \
  --work-it-out \
  --calculate "Date=Date(), ModifiedBy='CLI Import'"

items.name: eval
items.description: Evaluates MWScript expressions on the MoneyWorks server. Useful for calculations, queries, and testing MoneyWorks scripting.
items.example:
# Simple calculations
mw eval "1 + 1"
# Result: 2

# Count records
mw eval "Count(TaxRate)"
# Result: 5

# Date calculations
mw eval "Date() + 30"
# Result: 20250214

# Complex queries
mw eval "Sum(Transaction.Total, Period=202501 and Status='Posted')"
# Result: 45678.90

# String operations
mw eval "Uppercase('hello world')"
# Result: HELLO WORLD

items.name: version
items.description: Displays the MoneyWorks server version.
items.example:
mw version
# MoneyWorks version: 9.1.7

# With timing
mw version --timing
# MoneyWorks version: 9.1.7
# Timing: 45ms

items.name: list
items.description: Lists all available documents on the MoneyWorks server that the current user can access.
items.example:
mw list
# Available documents:
# 1. Acme Widgets
# 2. Demo Company
# 3. Test Database

# With debug info
mw list --debug
# [DEBUG] Connecting to localhost:6710
# [DEBUG] Fetching document list
# Available documents:
# ...

items.name: Export Options
items.description: Options available for the export command to control filtering, formatting, and output.
items.example:
# Filter options
--filter <expression>    # MoneyWorks filter expression
--search <text>         # Search text fields

# Sorting and pagination
--orderBy <field>       # Sort by field (e.g., "TaxCode DESC")
--limit <number>        # Limit records returned
--offset <number>       # Skip first n records

# Format options
--exportFormat <format> # Output format: compact, compact-headers, full, schema
--format <format>       # MW format: json, xml-terse, xml-verbose, tsv

# Output
--output <file>         # Save to file instead of stdout

items.name: Import Options
items.description: Options for controlling import behavior and data processing.
items.example:
# Required
--file <path>           # JSON file to import

# Import behavior
--mode <mode>           # Import mode: create, update, createOrUpdate
--work-it-out           # Enable automatic field resolution
--calculate <fields>    # Calculate field values (comma-separated)

# Examples:
--mode update
--calculate "Date=Date(), User='Import'"

items.name: Global Options
items.description: Global options that can be used with any command for configuration and debugging.
items.example:
# Configuration
--config <file>     # Path to config file (default: mw-config.json)

# Output control
--debug            # Enable debug mode with verbose logging
--timing           # Show timing information

# Help
--help             # Display help for command
--version          # Show CLI version

exports.name: Configuration
exports.description: Configuration file format and environment variables

items.name: Config File
items.description: JSON configuration file for MoneyWorks connection settings. The CLI looks for this file in the current directory by default.

items.name: Environment Variables
items.description: Environment variables used when no config file is specified. Useful for CI/CD pipelines and containerized deployments.
items.example:
# Connection settings
export MW_HOST=server.example.com
export MW_PORT=6710
export MW_USERNAME=Admin
export MW_PASSWORD=secure-password
export MW_DATAFILE="Production Data"
export MW_TIMEOUT=30000
export MW_VERSION=9

# Usage
mw export Account  # Uses environment variables

# Override specific values
MW_DATAFILE="Test Data" mw test-connection

items.name: loadConfig
items.description: Loads configuration from file or environment variables. Used internally by the CLI but exposed for programmatic use.

exports.name: Export Formats
exports.description: Available export format options with examples

items.name: compact
items.description: Exports data as raw arrays without field names. Most efficient for data transfer and storage.

items.name: compact-headers
items.description: Exports data as arrays with field names in the first row. Self-documenting while maintaining efficiency.

items.name: full
items.description: Default format. Exports data as complete JSON objects with field names. Best for development and data manipulation.

items.name: schema
items.description: Exports data with complete schema information including field types, constraints, and metadata. Ideal for documentation and LLM consumption.

exports.name: Types
exports.description: TypeScript types used by the CLI

items.name: CommandHandler
items.signature: type CommandHandler = (client: SmartMoneyWorksClient, args: string[], options: GlobalOptions) => Promise<void>
items.description: Function signature for CLI command handlers. All commands must implement this interface.

items.name: GlobalOptions
items.signature: interface GlobalOptions { config?: string; debug?: boolean; timing?: boolean; }
items.description: Global options available to all commands.

items.name: ExportOptions
items.description: Options specific to the export command.
items.example:
interface ExportOptions extends GlobalOptions {
  filter?: string;         // MoneyWorks filter expression
  search?: string;         // Text search
  orderBy?: string;        // Sort order
  limit?: number;          // Record limit
  offset?: number;         // Skip records
  exportFormat?: ExportFormat;  // Output format
  format?: string;         // MoneyWorks format
  output?: string;         // Output file path
}

items.name: ImportOptions
items.description: Options specific to the import command.

items.name: formatOutput
items.description: Formats data according to specified export format. Used internally by export command.

items.name: parseFilter
items.description: Validates and normalizes MoneyWorks filter expressions.

items.name: timing
items.description: Utility for timing operations. Automatically outputs timing when --timing flag is set.

examples.title: Basic Workflow
examples.description: Common workflow for exporting, modifying, and importing data
examples.code:
# 1. Test connection
mw test-connection

# 2. Export current data as backup
mw export TaxRate --output backup-taxrates.json

# 3. Export specific records to modify
mw export TaxRate --filter "Rate1 = 10" --output gst10-rates.json

# 4. Modify the JSON file (external process)
# ... edit gst10-rates.json ...

# 5. Import modified data
mw import TaxRate --file gst10-rates.json --mode update

# 6. Verify changes
mw export TaxRate --filter "Rate1 = 10.5"

examples.title: Automation Script
examples.description: Bash script for automated daily exports
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# daily-export.sh - Export all critical data

DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)
OUTPUT_DIR="backups/$DATE"

# Create output directory
mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

# Export all tables with timing
TABLES=("TaxRate" "Account" "Transaction" "Name" "Product")

for table in "${TABLES[@]}"; do
  echo "Exporting $table..."
  mw export "$table" \
    --output "$OUTPUT_DIR/${table}.json" \
    --exportFormat full \
    --timing
done

# Create compressed archive
tar -czf "backups/backup-$DATE.tar.gz" "$OUTPUT_DIR"
rm -rf "$OUTPUT_DIR"

echo "Backup complete: backups/backup-$DATE.tar.gz"

examples.title: Data Validation Pipeline
examples.description: Using CLI with jq for data validation and transformation
examples.code:
# Export and validate tax rates
mw export TaxRate --exportFormat full | jq '
  # Validate all rates are between 0 and 100
  map(select(.Rate1 < 0 or .Rate1 > 100)) |
  if length > 0 then
    error("Invalid tax rates found: \(.)")
  else
    "All tax rates valid" | {status: ., count: length}
  end
'

# Transform and filter accounts
mw export Account | jq '
  # Get income accounts with balance
  map(select(.Type == "Income" and .Balance > 0)) |
  map({code: .AccountCode, name: .Description, balance: .Balance}) |
  sort_by(.balance) | reverse
' > income-accounts.json

# Count transactions by period
mw export Transaction --filter 'Type="Invoice"' | jq '
  group_by(.Period) |
  map({period: .[0].Period, count: length, total: (map(.Total) | add)}) |
  sort_by(.period)
'

examples.title: Error Handling and Recovery
examples.description: Robust import with error handling and rollback
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# safe-import.sh - Import with automatic rollback on failure

TABLE="TaxRate"
IMPORT_FILE="new-rates.json"
BACKUP_FILE="backup-$(date +%s).json"

# Function to check if import was successful
check_import() {
  local count=$(mw eval "Count($TABLE)" 2>/dev/null)
  if [[ -z "$count" || "$count" -eq 0 ]]; then
    return 1
  fi
  return 0
}

# Create backup
echo "Creating backup..."
if ! mw export "$TABLE" --output "$BACKUP_FILE"; then
  echo "Failed to create backup. Aborting."
  exit 1
fi

# Perform import
echo "Importing from $IMPORT_FILE..."
if ! mw import "$TABLE" --file "$IMPORT_FILE" --mode createOrUpdate; then
  echo "Import failed. Attempting rollback..."
  
  # Rollback
  if mw import "$TABLE" --file "$BACKUP_FILE" --mode createOrUpdate; then
    echo "Rollback successful."
  else
    echo "CRITICAL: Rollback failed! Manual intervention required."
    echo "Backup file: $BACKUP_FILE"
    exit 2
  fi
  exit 1
fi

# Verify import
if ! check_import; then
  echo "Import verification failed. Check data integrity."
  exit 1
fi

echo "Import successful."
# Clean up backup after successful import
# rm "$BACKUP_FILE"

examples.title: Performance Testing
examples.description: Benchmark different export formats and options
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# benchmark.sh - Compare export performance

echo "MoneyWorks CLI Export Benchmark"
echo "================================"
echo ""

# Test different formats
FORMATS=("compact" "compact-headers" "full" "schema")
TABLE="Transaction"
LIMIT=1000

for format in "${FORMATS[@]}"; do
  echo "Testing format: $format"
  
  # Run 3 times and calculate average
  total_time=0
  for i in {1..3}; do
    # Capture timing output
    output=$(mw export "$TABLE" \
      --limit $LIMIT \
      --exportFormat "$format" \
      --timing 2>&1 >/dev/null)
    
    # Extract timing
    time=$(echo "$output" | grep -oE 'Total: ([0-9]+)ms' | grep -oE '[0-9]+')
    total_time=$((total_time + time))
    
    echo "  Run $i: ${time}ms"
  done
  
  avg=$((total_time / 3))
  echo "  Average: ${avg}ms"
  echo ""
done

# Test pagination performance
echo "Testing pagination (offset impact)"
for offset in 0 1000 5000 10000; do
  output=$(mw export "$TABLE" \
    --limit 100 \
    --offset $offset \
    --timing 2>&1 >/dev/null)
  
  time=$(echo "$output" | grep -oE 'Total: ([0-9]+)ms' | grep -oE '[0-9]+')
  echo "  Offset $offset: ${time}ms"
done

importPatterns.description: Run CLI without installation using bunx
Import configuration utilities for programmatic use
Import types for custom command development

designPrinciples.description: The CLI prioritizes ease of use with sensible defaults, clear error messages, and helpful debug output. Configuration via files or environment variables provides flexibility without complexity.
Supporting four distinct export formats ensures the CLI can serve different use cases - from efficient data transfer (compact) to rich documentation (schema).
The CLI respects MoneyWorks conventions, using exact field names and supporting native filter expressions and MWScript evaluation.
Designed to work well with Unix pipes and scripting, enabling complex workflows through composition of simple commands.
Built-in timing and debug modes provide transparency into performance, helping developers optimize their integrations.

futurePlans.description: Add REPL-style interactive mode for exploratory data analysis
Show progress bars for large export/import operations
Stream large datasets to reduce memory usage
Support for all MoneyWorks tables (Account, Transaction, Name, etc.)
Preview import changes without applying them
Monitor and sync changes between local files and MoneyWorks
